title: Council of Trent opens
summary: On 13 December 1545, the third Sunday of Advent, an ecumenical council of the Roman Catholic Church opened in the Alpine bishopric of Trento with a solemn mass sung by Cardinal Giovanni Maria del Monte and a sermon that quoted Isaiah. Perhaps thirty bishops and three papal legates attended the first session. Over three sittings spread across eighteen years the Council of Trent defined Catholic doctrine against the Protestant Reformation, decreed sweeping reform of clergy and worship, and set the pattern for a militant, disciplined Roman Church that would last until the Second Vatican Council four hundred years later.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
For a generation before 1545 the demand for a general council had been the constant refrain of everyone unhappy with the state of Latin Christendom. Erasmus of Rotterdam had called for one in his Enchiridion; Emperor Charles V, whose Holy Roman Empire was fracturing along confessional lines, believed a council was the only way to reconcile Lutheran princes to Rome. Martin Luther himself, at the Diet of Worms in 1521 and again in his Appeal to the German Nobility, appealed to a free general council above the pope. But successive popes in the 1520s and 1530s postponed, and postponed again, fearing conciliarism, French obstruction, and a loss of Roman control.

The political landscape was fraught. Pope Clement VII, sacked in his own city by mutinous imperial troops in 1527, spent his last years dodging Charles V's insistence on a council; his successor Paul III, the Farnese pope elected in 1534, was more sympathetic. Paul convened a preliminary commission of reform in 1537 whose report, the Consilium de emendanda ecclesia, catalogued the abuses of curia and clergy with brutal honesty. Two attempts at a council, first at Mantua in 1537 and then at Vicenza in 1538, collapsed under French vetoes and imperial travel restrictions.

The city eventually chosen was Trento, a small prince-bishopric on the Adige just inside the Alps, formally an imperial territory but Italian-speaking and reachable by both German and Italian bishops. Trento suited Charles V because it lay in his empire; it suited Paul III because it was close enough to Rome that legates and mail could reach it quickly. A first attempt to open in 1542 failed when the outbreak of the Italian War of 1542 to 1546 prevented most bishops from travelling. When peace was patched up at Crépy in September 1544 Paul III issued a new bull of convocation, Laetare Ierusalem, and the council was finally set for the third Sunday of Advent 1545.

What opened on 13 December was small: three papal legates (Del Monte, Marcello Cervini, and the English cardinal Reginald Pole), four archbishops, twenty-one bishops, five superiors of religious orders, and about fifty theologians and canonists in advisory capacity. There were no Protestant delegates, no Orthodox observers, and only a handful of French bishops. But the mandate was vast: to define Catholic doctrine against the Reformation, to reform clerical morals and discipline, and, if possible, to launch a crusade against the Turks.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The council met in three widely separated periods over eighteen years. The first period, 1545 to 1547, was held under Paul III and did most of the doctrinal foundation work. Its early decrees, hammered out in the palace of the Prince-Bishop and the cathedral of San Vigilio, were decisive. On 8 April 1546 the fourth session decreed that the sources of divine revelation were 'Sacred Scripture and unwritten traditions' received from the apostles, both to be venerated 'with equal pious affection.' It authorised the Latin Vulgate of Jerome as the standard biblical text and included the deuterocanonical books rejected by the reformers. On 13 January 1547 the sixth session issued a lengthy decree on justification: salvation depended on prevenient grace, human cooperation with grace, and could be increased or lost by works, a careful refutation of Luther's sola fide.

In March 1547, spooked by a typhus outbreak and by pressure from Paul III to move to a safer city, the papal legates transferred the council to Bologna. Charles V, furious at losing a council on his own imperial soil, ordered the imperial bishops to stay in Trento; the Bologna sessions accomplished little and were suspended in 1549. Paul III died the same year, and his successor, the former legate Del Monte, took the throne as Julius III.

The second period, 1551 to 1552, reconvened at Trento on 1 May 1551. It produced the great decrees on the Eucharist, penance, and extreme unction: transubstantiation, real presence, sacramental confession, and priestly absolution were all defined against Protestant challenges. For the first and only time a small Protestant delegation, sent by the Duke of Württemberg, arrived at Trento in January 1552 but refused to submit to the council's authority and left. The council was suspended in April 1552 as the Elector Maurice of Saxony's rebellion swept across southern Germany, forcing Charles V to flee Innsbruck and the bishops to disperse.

A decade passed. It took the diplomatic patience of Pope Pius IV, a Milanese lawyer elected in 1559, and the political muscle of his nephew Charles Borromeo, cardinal-secretary of state and archbishop of Milan, to reopen the council in January 1562. This third period lasted almost two years, drew a much larger attendance including sixty-eight bishops on the opening day, and pushed through the reform decrees that gave Trent its lasting shape. On 4 December 1563 the twenty-fifth and final session issued decrees on purgatory, the invocation of saints, relics, sacred images (with cautions against superstition, aimed at Protestant iconoclasts), religious orders, and indulgences. The bishops present kissed the papal legate's hand, sang a Te Deum, and dispersed.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Pius IV confirmed the decrees by the bull Benedictus Deus on 26 January 1564 and reserved to Rome the right to interpret them. From that year on, no local synod, university, or bishop could reopen the definitions of Trent; the papal Congregation of the Council, established in 1564, would police their application. A stream of instruments followed: the Roman Catechism of 1566, aimed at parish priests, translated the decrees into pastoral form; the reformed Roman Breviary of 1568 and Roman Missal of 1570 imposed a single Latin liturgy, the Tridentine Mass, on almost the entire Latin Church and lasted until the reforms of the Second Vatican Council in the 1960s. The Congregation of the Index issued its first list of banned books in 1564.

Diocesan reform, driven by Charles Borromeo in Milan and imitated across Catholic Europe, gave the parish clergy proper seminary training for the first time. Trent had required each diocese to establish a seminary; the model spread slowly but steadily, and by the seventeenth century a Tridentine curate was recognisable everywhere from Poland to Peru: celibate, tonsured, disciplined, and better educated than his medieval predecessor. Bishops were required to reside in their dioceses, to visit their parishes, and to hold synods. Cathedral chapters lost their long autonomy. The Jesuits, founded by Ignatius of Loyola in 1540 and unmentioned in most Tridentine decrees, became the intellectual shock troops of the reforms.

The political consequences were as sharp. Trent formally rejected reconciliation with Protestants and treated their doctrines as heresies to be extirpated. Confessional lines hardened across Europe; the Wars of Religion in France (1562 to 1598), the Dutch Revolt (from 1568), and eventually the Thirty Years' War (1618 to 1648) can all be read in part as the political working out of Trent's definitions. Catholic missionaries carried Tridentine Christianity to New Spain, the Andes, the Congo, and the Ming and Tokugawa courts; the Franciscans, Dominicans, and Jesuits between them built parishes, colleges, and hospitals from Manila to Quito. But Trent also drew a boundary: after 1564 the Catholic and Protestant worlds would evolve as separate ecclesiastical and cultural systems, each with its own catechism, its own art, and its own memory of the sixteenth century.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Trent shaped Catholic life for four centuries. Its liturgical decrees produced the celebrations of Mass that most Catholic grandparents alive today remember from their childhood: the Latin, the eastward-facing altar, the ceremonial silence at the consecration, the priest as sacrificing minister. Its doctrinal formulations, codified in the Tridentine Profession of Faith, remained the standard rule of belief; Vatican I in 1870, which defined papal infallibility, and Vatican II from 1962 to 1965, which reformed liturgy and ecclesiology, both worked within a Tridentine frame even as Vatican II moved beyond it.

For cultural historians the more subtle legacy is Trent's role in creating what Jean Delumeau and Peter Burke have called the confessionalisation of Europe. State and church combined to catechise populations, enforce doctrinal uniformity, discipline morals, and produce the distinctive Baroque devotional culture of pilgrimage, relics, rosary, and Marian apparitions that flourished from Loreto to Guadalupe. The very art of the Counter-Reformation, from Caravaggio's naturalism to Bernini's Ecstasy of Saint Teresa, took shape under Tridentine sensibilities about clarity, decorum, and the affective power of holy images.

Protestant polemic long portrayed Trent as a monument to obscurantism, and even sympathetic Catholic historians such as Hubert Jedin, whose four-volume History of the Council of Trent published between 1949 and 1975 remains the standard work, have conceded the missed ecumenical opportunity of the 1540s. But recent scholarship has emphasised the council's genuine achievements in clerical reform, its serious theological engagement with the reformers' concerns, and its ambition to make Catholicism a lived faith of catechised laypeople rather than mere ritual. The joint Lutheran-Catholic Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification, signed in Augsburg in 1999, marked one of the first formal steps toward healing the doctrinal wound that Trent had helped to open in 1547.

worldContext.text: In New Spain the Franciscan and Dominican missions were in full spread across central Mexico; the Colegio de Santa Cruz de Tlatelolco, teaching Latin and theology to sons of the Nahua nobility, had opened in 1536. In Peru the civil wars among conquistadors were finally winding down after the execution of Gonzalo Pizarro in 1548. The great silver mountain of Potosí was struck in 1545, the year Trent opened; within a decade its refineries were feeding the mints that would send bullion across the Atlantic and, via the Manila galleon after 1571, across the Pacific to Ming China.

The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent, in his fifth decade of rule, dominated the eastern Mediterranean; his admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa had died in 1546, and Ottoman fleets were harrying the Italian coast throughout the 1540s and 1550s. In 1555 the Peace of Amasya ended thirty years of Ottoman-Safavid war and stabilised the border across Iraq and the Caucasus. Cairo was one of the great cities of the Muslim world, still recovering from the shock of Ottoman conquest in 1517 but flourishing as an entrepôt for pilgrims, spices, and coffee.

In Ming China the Jiajing Emperor was in the middle of his forty-five-year reign, absorbed in Daoist alchemy and increasingly detached from government; coastal wokou piracy peaked in the 1550s. In Sengoku Japan the daimyō Oda Nobunaga was a boy of ten in 1545; matchlock firearms brought by Portuguese in 1543 were already changing warfare. Francis Xavier, the Basque Jesuit, would land at Kagoshima in August 1549 and open a Catholic mission that grew rapidly until the Tokugawa persecutions of the seventeenth century.

In north India the Afghan Sur dynasty under Islam Shah Suri ruled the sultanate his father Sher Shah Suri had rebuilt on the ashes of Humayun's Mughal state; Humayun himself was in Persian exile at the court of Shah Tahmasp I. His infant son Akbar was being raised in Kabul. Vijayanagara, the great Hindu empire of the Deccan, was at the height of its wealth under Achyuta Deva Raya and would suffer its catastrophic defeat at Talikota in 1565. Portuguese Goa remained the hub of the Estado da India.

In Ethiopia the young emperor Gelawdewos, having defeated Ahmad Gragn's jihad in 1543 with Portuguese help, was rebuilding a Christian kingdom under intense pressure from Oromo migrations from the south. In West Africa the Songhai Empire under Askia Ishaq I still dominated the Niger bend, though the trans-Saharan trade was being eroded by the growing Atlantic slave trade. The Kingdom of Kongo, formally Catholic since 1491, had passed to King Diogo I after the death of Afonso I; Portuguese slavers were now shipping enslaved Africans to Brazil in growing numbers.

No European ship had yet reached Australia, New Zealand, or most Pacific islands. Spanish and Portuguese charts speculated about a great southern continent, Terra Australis, and Iñigo Ortiz de Retez sailing from Tidore in 1545 gave the name New Guinea to the island he sighted. Māori communities in Aotearoa were entering the classic phase of their culture, with fortified pā and intensive kūmara horticulture; Polynesian voyaging traditions continued to link Tonga, Samoa, Hawaii, and Rapa Nui, and Aboriginal nations across Australia sustained their long practices of Country management.

figures.name: Pope Paul III
figures.role: Farnese pope who convoked the council and presided over its first period 1545 to 1547

figures.name: Pope Pius IV
figures.role: Milanese pope who reconvened the council in 1562 and confirmed its decrees in 1564

figures.name: Cardinal Giovanni Maria del Monte
figures.role: First presiding legate at Trent, later elected as Pope Julius III

figures.name: Charles Borromeo
figures.role: Cardinal-nephew of Pius IV who drove the third session's reforms and modelled the Tridentine bishop

figures.name: Emperor Charles V
figures.role: Habsburg emperor whose insistence forced the popes to convene a council on imperial soil

images.caption: The Council of Trent, fresco painted by the Roman mannerist Pasquale Cati around 1588 for the Altemps chapel in Santa Maria in Trastevere, showing the bishops in session while allegorical figures of the Church triumph in the foreground.
images.credit: Pasquale Cati (1550 to 1620), via Wikimedia Commons
